speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. The survivor's name is Walter Absil, born Bondy-- W-A-L-T-E-R A-B-S-I-L B-O-N-D-Y. Interviewer is Judy Breuer-- J-U-D-Y B-R-E-U-E-R. The interview is being conducted in Toronto, Canada. Language of the interview is English. Today is August 6, 1996. The survivor is Walter Absil, born Bondy. The interviewer is Judy Breuer. And the interview is being conducted in Toronto, Canada. The language is English. When were you-- what is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Walter Absil.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born, Mr. Absil?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna in 26 November, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe Vienna to me, Mr. Absil, during the years when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Vienna was probably one of the leading cities in Europe. You had Paris. And you probably had London and Vienna. Vienna was known for its-- the medicine and the doctors. People came from all over the world. This was a center, not only of the arts, of the painters, but also of the leading medicine of that day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived-- the life I recall was very pleasant. My parents were not rich. They had a small manufacture of leather belts. And while you cannot become wealthy doing that, you also live quite well. I remember that we did have a maid that walked us in the parks. And we also had someone help my mother with the kitchen. So we really had two employees. We had a seat at the opera. And I would say we were middle class people. And we were like considering ourselves as Austria's first and only Jews in a secondary way. We were not very religious. We went to synagogue on Yom Kippur. And I did make my bar mitzvah in Vienna. But besides that, we really did not belong to the orthodox group of Jews that were primarily refugees from Poland that reached German-speaking countries like Austria after the First World War or during the First World War. Those poor people had to be sustained by the Jewish Committee of Vienna. And they were dressed in their religious garbs-- black hats and black clothes. And the Austrians really didn't like them very much. Because they, they were strange to them. And they also knew that they were Jews. And if there is one place in this world that could be considered the birth place of the Holocaust, it certainly was the city of Vienna, where a down and out Adolf Hitler, he lived in very bad conditions in, in some men's homes where the unemployed lived. He tried to sell his paintings. That didn't work. They threw him out of the academy of, of, of the painters. They-- he tried to, to learn how to paint. And then they said he had no talent. And they threw him out of there. But he then saw how the mayor of the city of Vienna got elected every time on an anti-Semitic ticket and that being an outspoken anti-Semite would be a political asset for him. He also read the writings and rantings of famous anti-Semites in Austria. One of them was called [INAUDIBLE]. And he published a pamphlet, a anti-Semitic pamphlet. That's where Hitler learned his craft. And eventually, then he moved on to Germany and became a soldier in the German Wehrmacht. And they eventually locked him up in prison at Lansberg. And while he was in prison, he wrote his famous

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to go back a little bit to the beginning of our interview. We initially started to talk about Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I would like to know is you walked the streets of Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you were growing up? Please, could you describe to me the Danube, described to me the architecture, the people who walked the streets of Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Vienna has escaped to a great extent the bombardments of other major cities of Europe. The only major damage downtown was the, the great cathedral, which can only be compared to the one in Cologne. And while that tremendous church built in the 11th century was not damaged by bombs, but it was burned by the, by the SS. When the SS falsely heard reports that the dome in Cologne had been bombed, they burnt the St. Stephen's Cathedral in Vienna. The was-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the ratio of Jews and Gentiles of Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna a city of about two million people. And we had about 200,000 Jews in Vienna. Half of those 200,000 Jews were assimilated Jews like ourselves that considered themselves Austrian first. And then you had the poor-- as I mentioned to you-- the, the, the refugees that reached German-speaking countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to talk about the refugees. When did these refugees started coming into Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if you recall your history of the First World War, Hindenburg, who was even then an aging leader of-- general of the German army won a tremendous battle, the Battle of Tannenberg, which was fought very close to the Russian-Polish border. Those battles were raging back and forth. I don't have to tell you that when troops occupy a shtetl in Poland, the first thing that the, the whatever soldiers just conquered this village, the first thing they do is rape and steal and rob the local populations. So those poor people fled the, the battles of the First World War and reached German-speaking countries like Austria or Germany. Second, their emotion was that they-- when they forced Jews into the Polish army, those guys didn't really survive very long. Besides having the enemy to fight, they also had their Polish comrades to fight. If they saw a Jew, they more or less killed him. So those people, in order to escape the draft, fled Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: They settled in Vienna. How did-- you mentioned that the Viennese didn't really like the Polish Jews because they were so different. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What I would like to know is how did the Austrian Jews feel about the Polish refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you asked first about the Viennese. The Viennese did not like Jews, period. 1,000 years of church rantings against the Christ killers had really educated the Austrian masses. The only difference between those Polish Jews in Vienna and ourselves was that we were not easily recognizable. We really could pass for Austrians. And we were not discriminated against, because people didn't know. If you meet me or my dad on the street, you didn't know that we were Jewish. Well, those people stuck out like a sore thumb. And so the wrath of the Austrian population was really against those foreigners that, besides being dressed funny, spoke funny. And they spoke some Yiddish. But the Yiddish was certainly not close to the Viennese accent. And the-- you asked me about the Austrian Jewish community. Well, they kind of look down at those strangers. And, and, and of course the, the Jewish Committee helped. They gave them some money. And they had some field kitchens. I remember that those people were standing in line in order to get some soup. And we, we kind of looked down at them like if they were some kind of second rate citizens. They were not citizens at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't associate with them at all, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not associate. I did not go to school with them. I remember that in my class, we had a few Jews, I believe six or seven. And the rest where Austrians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live in a different section of Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: They certainly did. They lived in the second district of Vienna, which had the nickname of Mazzesinsel, which means Matzo's Island. And that's where they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they have their own synagogues and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They have their own synagogues, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And stores and schools--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, stores or stands at the markets. And they were peddling goods. And one of the, of those people that became famous in Canada is the Reichmann family. They come from Hungary near the Austrian border. And they were selling Hungarian chickens in Vienna. And because in those days a religious Jew could not enter a bank and ask them to, to change Austrian Schilling, which they had gotten for their chickens, into forint or-- they had to get into the money changing business in order to go back to Hungary and buy more chickens. And that's how they eventually got into the money changing business, through eggs and chickens. Another interesting part of the Reichmann family-- I don't know if that's of interest to you-- is that when the Germans marched in, and they being Hungarian subjects, even though they were religious Jews, the Germans didn't touch them because they were not Austrians. They were Hungarians. And when the Reichmanns eventually immigrated to Paris and they had to fill out a form. How much money they took with them-- and they were quite wealthy even then. And Mr. Reichmann marked on the paper, I'm sorry. I can't figure that one out, playing dumb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Absil, I would like to talk about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was called Otto-- O-T-T-O, Otto Bondy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1897.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna. He, he was kind of a trombenik, if you know what I mean. He was all-- he was a, a joker. He always had a laughing face. But I recall he always was good for a practical joke. I remember him smearing somebody that had a, a sunburn. He told them that rubbing sand on it would help him. I have to tell you that this didn't help very much. I remember that once my uncle fell asleep on the street car. And when they came to their own stop, he told the fellow, let them sleep. And by the time my uncle woke up, he was three miles down the road. Those are the kind of practical jokes that he played.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever talk to you about his experiences during the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I know that he was under the Italian front, that they had-- while he was not hurt, he certainly told me that they had a lot of hardships, tremendous cold-- those winters in the mountains on, on the Italian front. They didn't have much to eat. I think that whatever wasn't nailed down in different farms, they stole, and bread or cheese or whatever. And they kind of had to nourish themselves. Why they didn't get supplied by the Austrian army, I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father support his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, he, he dabbled in leather goods. And he bought hides and cut those hides. I remember those hides being on, on the piano in the parlor. We didn't have a warehouse as such. And he cut them into strips. And then we had some women that came. And he gave him buckles. And they then would sew on the buckles. And we would package or somebody would package the goods. And then my dad himself went to the stores and sold some belts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was his supplier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he must have gone to some hide wholesalers. I have no idea. Don't forget that was-- when we left Austria, I was 13. And I went to school. But I wouldn't remember where he bought his hides or his buckles. I couldn't tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been a successful business in order to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very small business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was his business? Was it in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in our home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your house to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- when I was born, apartments were at the premium. You couldn't really get any. But we did have of wealthy uncle. And he owned an apartment building in the Third District of Vienna. And through this connection, we, we managed to obtain the apartment we lived in. We-- it was quite a large apartment. There was a kitchen. There was a bathroom, in those days a luxury. There was the main master bedroom. There was a children's room where my sister and I slept. And then there was a dining room that had a large build-in what they call [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you, you heard of that. They are built on the wall. You can't remove those. They still use them in Austria and Germany. And then we have the parlor where the piano was and the merchandise. Class It was quite a large apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in your house like music?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my dad was an aficionado of, of classical music. And while my mother also liked music, she didn't know as much about, about the classics than my dad did. But she was playing the piano. And for my dad's birthday, she would surprise him with a new air that she had practiced in secret. And that was his birthday present, that she could play another song or another air.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with your father when he went to the opera or the symphony?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have a seat at the opera for a certain time. At the end, I don't think we had it anymore. And yes, I do remember-- probably I was 10, 11-- when my first opera was I went to the theater and saw some German pieces and, you know. It wasn't Shakespeare for sure. But I think it was Kleist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not, no. And he came from a family that was absolutely not religious. And also, my mother's family was not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since he didn't come from a religious family, your mother didn't come from a religious family, how did you identify as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, when I went to school, you had to identify yourself as a Israelite. In those days, we weren't called Jews were called-- the religion Israelitish, which is Israelite. We had to go to a, a cheder, religious school. I remember very small kind of room where we had lessons, Hebrew lessons. And also, that's where I learnt my-- to study for my bar mitzvah. And I made my bar mitzvah in, in December probably of-- oh, it probably was the-- February, 1938. And that was the last time our family was together, all, all the different branches of our family. Because in March, 1938, the, the Germans marched in. And we had the familiar, famous Anschluss.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to talk about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: We talked about your father already. How would you describe your mother to me? First of all, when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in 1904. She was the oldest daughter of, of her parents. She was the first born. And she was a kind of a soft person. What was her name? Her name was Margarethe. But we called her Gretel. Everybody called her Greta, Gretel, which is kind of a, an endearment in, in Austria, short for Margarethe. And besides playing the piano, she-- I think she was knitting also. And she was a homemaker primarily. Every summer, we went to, to the country somewhere. We rented a, a, a house or an apartment in the country. And like-- like I said, life wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy reading poetry books?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that my first books were [INAUDIBLE], which is a classic. It's something like Cooper, who type of things, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who bought these books for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess my parents bought those books for me. And the first edition of Shakespeare, I got from my [INAUDIBLE], my bar mitzvah. But I'm afraid that that book was left behind. I never read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one younger sister and two and 1/2 year younger than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: One sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liesl-- again, a shortened. But that, that's the way she-- it says in her birth certificate, Liesl, like the girl in

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start school, at what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess that you went to first grade school, just like here. I don't remember what age exactly. But I did four years of, of public school. And from then, I went to what's called a gymnasium over there, which is kind of a high school, an intermediate high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were these schools located, near your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the, the, the first one was very near home at the end of the street. There was a little square with a church and, and a boys and a girls school. I went to one side. My sister went to the other side. It was in walking distance. And even the, the gymnasium was just a little bit further around the corner. And I walked to that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any specific interests in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I was the troublemaker again. I remember removing the stops from the piano and, and the piano while the teacher sat down and tried to roll it into the classroom. I remember the police came when we were spitting on the head of a policeman from the third floor. So troublemaker, that was me. That's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you punished for all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. You mostly get away with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now on the serious side--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: The school you went to, did you have Gentile children there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yes. I told you we were about six, seven Jews in a class of about 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Gentile feel about the Jews and the Jews about the Gentiles in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow, I don't recall any ill feelings. As a matter of fact, I was friend with one Gentile boy. But my best friend was another Jewish boy. A funny thing happened. You know, the Third District of Vienna recently had an ad in the, in the Jewish paper asking for people that used to live in that area and asking if they would report to them. And they wanted to find out what happened to a Jewish neighbor, as if they didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote to them. And I sent them some documents. And lo and behold, a few months later, I get a letter from Vienna from some fellow that says, I went to school with you in that gymnasium. And I say, how is that possible? How would he remember he went to school with me? And I changed my name. It's impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Vienna. I wrote him that we will be at such a hotel on such a date. And there, he came along. And the first thing he did, he shoved a picture in my face. And there sitting in the first row-- he was absolutely right. And he's a half Jew. And he survived in Vienna. His mother was a Catholic. And he was baptized. And he passed for a, for a Mischling, for a mixed Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: You got me. But I can find out for you. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun and amusement when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went swimming. And we went-- I had a bicycle and went to, to movies, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what little boys do, I guess-- nothing exciting, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get ready for your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I told you, I went to this little cheder. And with a lot of, lot of help and pushing by my mother, I, I made my bar mitzvah in, in the synagogue that has been restored, the only synagogue in Vienna that was not demolished because it was inside an apartment building. And while the Nazis trashed it and destroyed everything inside, they could not burn the building because Gentiles lived above it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of this synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Its the [INAUDIBLE] temple, which, which really translate as the little-- the side street temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your bar mitzvah celebration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I do recall that when we went back to the synagogue that had been restored recently, the Austrian government paid for that restoration. It looked exactly the, the way I remember it. It has a blue ceiling. It's kind of an oval synagogue. The whole shape of the, the room is oval. And the canopy has gold stars and has, has a blue ceiling on it. It's a very old fashioned day. The women, for example, are not on the ground floor. The women have to walk upstairs and kind of very narrow stairs. And they have to sit upstairs, which is something my wife complained immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the celebration like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it was very simple. We, we-- I guess I got a blue suit. And I read from the Haftorah. And we then went to one of the famous ring cafes. You see, the, the center of Vienna where the walls of the city once stood has a large avenue encircling the center. And under this avenue, you have a lot of cafes where you can sit all day with one cup of coffee and read all the newspapers. And that's what the Viennese do. They sit and read the newspapers. You don't go-- you don't buy your newspaper. You buy a cup of coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Absil, previously, we talked about the rise of Hitler, how he happened to become the leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: Learn his trade in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Learn his trade. How did the rise of Hitler in Germany affect your life? I believe it was 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as long as the Nazis only came to power in Germany, everybody thought that this was only a passing fancy, that he wouldn't be last. And in Austria, oh, we did have a lot of Nazis. And they looked towards Germany. But we kind of discounted his power. And as long as he didn't march into Austria, of course, we were not in danger whatsoever. And we didn't believe that it would come about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know any Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of no, no. We did have, we did have someone that-- you know what an [INAUDIBLE] is, an Aryan? And we had one fellow that worked for us. Now, what did he do for us, I really don't know. Was he selling some of my dad's goods? Or was he supplying the leather? I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he took over as an Aryan administrator. I don't believe he was a Nazi, because he worked with Jews all the time. But then he kind of became the big [NON-ENGLISH], The, the Austrians were opportunistic. They grasped this occasion where you could take things away from the Jews. You didn't have to be a Nazi to do that. You, you, you took the occasion to enrich yourself and figure what the hell? Everybody-- Do you remember the name of this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ott, O-T-T, Franz Ott.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what became of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- after the liberation, I once went back to Vienna. And when I rang his bell, he mistook me for my dad. And he got scared stiff. But then I said, no, I'm not Otto. I'm, I'm his son. And he gave me a-- some gifts for the kids. And I walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just wanted to see. I also took some other belongings that I recognized. I took some carpets that I knew were mine, some books that I found. And he gave them to me. I could have with the police or with the, with, with the British soldiers or Russian soldiers, have him evacuated from that apartment and probably seize everything that was in there. But I didn't feel like doing that. And I didn't need an apartment in Vienna. I was living in Brussels, Belgium. So I simply took the items that I recognized. And I walked away.
